blocks.text: JavaScript can do math the same way a calculator can. You already know most of the symbols plus, minus, multiply, divide. There are five operators total, and each one has a job.

blocks.alt: Five cards showing the five math operators: + for add, - for subtract, * for multiply, / for divide, and % for remainder, each with a code example
blocks.caption: These five operators are the building blocks of all math in JavaScript.

blocks.label: One difference from math class
blocks.text: Multiplication uses * (not x) and division uses / (not the division symbol). Your keyboard doesn't have those symbols so JavaScript uses these instead.

A number on its own is fine for one calculation. But what if you need to use that number again later? Or change it? That's where variables come in. You store your numbers in variables, then use the variable names in your math. JavaScript swaps in the value automatically.

blocks.alt: Four step flowchart showing: declare variables with let, store numbers in them, use the variable names in a math expression, then log the result to the console
blocks.caption: You write the variable name JavaScript looks up the number stored inside it and uses that.

blocks.desc: JavaScript reads price (20) and tax (2), adds them, and stores 22 into total. You never have to type the actual numbers again just the names.

blocks.label: Why this matters
blocks.text: If you change price from 20 to 35, total automatically becomes 37 the next time it's calculated. You changed one variable and everything that depends on it updates. That's the whole point of variables.

The % operator is the one that looks strangest at first. It doesn't give you the result of dividing it gives you the leftover after dividing as evenly as possible. That leftover is called the remainder.

blocks.alt: Ten numbered dots grouped into three boxes of three, with one dot left over in a dashed box labeled 'left over'. The result is 1.
blocks.caption: 10 % 3 = 1 because 3 fits into 10 three full times, with 1 left over.

blocks.label: The even/odd trick
blocks.text: Any number % 2 gives you either 0 or 1. If the answer is 0, the number is even. If it's 1, the number is odd. Game developers use this constantly for example, to do something every other turn.

Updating a variable using itself is so common adding to a score, losing a life, collecting coins that JavaScript has shortcuts for it. Instead of writing the full thing every time, you can use a shorter version.

blocks.alt: Side by side table: the long way to write score = score + 10 versus the shortcut score += 10, repeated for -=, *=, ++, and --
blocks.caption: The shortcut does exactly the same thing it just saves you from typing the variable name twice.

blocks.desc: The most common shortcut of all adds exactly 1. You'll see this in almost every program that counts something.

blocks.text: JavaScript follows the same math rules you learned in school. Multiplication and division happen before addition and subtraction. If you want a different order, use parentheses they always run first.

blocks.alt: Two expressions side by side: 2 + 3 * 4 equals 14 because * runs first, versus (2 + 3) * 4 equals 20 because parentheses run first
blocks.caption: Same numbers, different answer all because of where the parentheses go.

blocks.label: When in doubt, use parentheses
blocks.text: If you're not sure what order JavaScript will evaluate your math, add parentheses to make it explicit. There's no penalty for using them and they make your intention obvious to anyone reading your code.
Here's a code block that uses everything from this lesson at once. Read through each line before running it try to predict what the final value of score will be.

blocks.label: Predict before you run
blocks.text: Before hitting Run, write down what you think score will be at the end. Then run it and compare. If you were wrong, trace through each line again this is the most important skill in programming.

exercises.title: Which operator does which job?
tasks: Which operator gives you the leftover after dividing?
exercises.hint: It's not + - * or /. It's the fifth one.
exercises.explanation: % is the remainder operator. 10 % 3 = 1 because 3 fits into 10 three full times, with 1 left over.

exercises.title: What does this print?
tasks: Read the code. What number does console.log print?
exercises.hint: What is 10 multiplied by 4?
exercises.explanation: 10 * 4 = 40. That value gets stored in x, and console.log prints whatever is inside x.

exercises.title: Match the expression to its result
tasks: Drag each expression into the bucket showing its result.
exercises.hint: Work through each one step by step. Remember % gives you the leftover after dividing.
exercises.explanation: 10 + 3 = 13, 10 * 2 = 20, 10 % 3 = 1 (3 goes into 10 three times with 1 left over), 10 - 3 = 7.

exercises.title: Drop the operator
tasks: Drop the correct operator into the blank to calculate the total cost.
exercises.hint: You're combining price and tax together. Which operator adds two things?
exercises.explanation: Adding price + tax gives the total cost. The other operators would give wrong results: - subtracts, * multiplies, / divides.

exercises.title: Drop the operator and the variable
tasks: Two blanks this time. Drop the operator and the second variable to calculate the area.
exercises.hint: Area = length times width. The operator for multiply is *. What is the second variable called?
exercises.explanation: length * width multiplies the two variables. Using the variable name 'width' instead of the raw number 4 means the formula still works if you change width later.

exercises.title: What does this print? (two variables)
tasks: Two variables are used in a math expression. What does the console print?
exercises.hint: JavaScript replaces each variable name with its value, then does the math.
exercises.explanation: JavaScript looks up apples (5) and oranges (3), adds them to get 8, and stores that in total. The console prints 8.

exercises.title: Type the operator
tasks: Type the operator that subtracts amountSpent from balance.
exercises.hint: You're taking money away. Which single character operator removes something?
exercises.explanation: balance - amountSpent = 50 - 12 = 38. The minus operator subtracts the right side from the left.

exercises.title: Type the operator and the variable
tasks: Two blanks now. Type the operator and the second variable name.
exercises.hint: You're multiplying price by quantity. One character for the operator, then the variable name.
exercises.explanation: price * quantity = 15 * 3 = 45. Using the variable name 'quantity' means the formula updates automatically if quantity changes.

exercises.title: Put the calculation in order
tasks: Drag these lines into the correct order so the math works.
exercises.hint: You have to declare a variable before you can use it in a calculation.
exercises.explanation: You must declare price and quantity before using them in the total calculation. Then log after total exists.

exercises.title: What does a shortcut mean?
tasks: What does score += 10 mean?
exercises.hint: The += shortcut is a shorter way of writing something you already know.

options.label: Create a new variable called score with value 10
Add 10 to score and store the result back in score

exercises.explanation: score += 10 is a shortcut for score = score + 10. It reads score, adds 10, and stores the result back. The variable updates itself.

exercises.title: What does this print? (shortcut)
tasks: A shortcut operator is used. What does the console print?
exercises.hint: score += 10 does the same thing as score = score + 10.
exercises.explanation: score starts at 5. score += 10 adds 10 to it, making it 15. The console prints 15.

exercises.title: Drop the shortcut
tasks: You lost a life. Drop the shortcut operator that subtracts 1 from lives.
exercises.hint: You want to subtract. The shortcut for subtracting is -=.
exercises.explanation: -= subtracts from a variable. lives -= 1 is the shortcut for lives = lives - 1. lives goes from 3 to 2.

exercises.title: Drop the shortcut and the amount
tasks: Two blanks. Drop the shortcut operator and the number to collect 10 coins.
exercises.hint: You're adding coins, so you're using +=. How many are you collecting?
exercises.explanation: coins += 10 adds 10 to coins. It's the shortcut for coins = coins + 10. coins goes from 0 to 10.

exercises.title: Type the shortcut
tasks: Rewrite the long version as a shortcut. Fill in the blank.
exercises.hint: score = score + 3 can be written as score ___ 3. The shortcut operator is two characters.
exercises.explanation: += is the shortcut for adding to a variable. score += 3 does exactly the same thing as score = score + 3.

exercises.title: Drop the full shortcut line
tasks: Every part of line 2 is missing. Drop all four tokens in the right order.
exercises.hint: A shortcut expression has: variable name, shortcut operator, number, semicolon is already there.
exercises.explanation: health -= 10 subtracts 10 from health. 100 - 10 = 90. The three parts are: the variable name, the shortcut operator, and the amount.

exercises.title: Type the shortcut and amount
tasks: No hints this time. Type both the shortcut operator and the amount yourself.
exercises.hint: You're subtracting 5 from health. The subtraction shortcut is two characters.
exercises.explanation: health -= 5 subtracts 5 from health. 100 - 5 = 95. No hints this time you wrote both parts from memory.

exercises.title: What does this print? (order of operations)
tasks: Two very similar expressions. What are the two values printed?
exercises.hint: In the first line, what runs first: the + or the *?
exercises.explanation: Without parentheses, * runs first: 3 * 4 = 12, then 2 + 12 = 14. With parentheses, + runs first: 2 + 3 = 5, then 5 * 4 = 20. Same numbers, different answers.

exercises.title: Run the math
tasks: The code is already written just hit Run.
Look at each number printed in the console.
Which result surprised you?
Change one of the numbers and run it again to see how the output changes.
exercises.hint: Just hit Run first. Read the output. Then start changing numbers.

checks.label: something is printed to the console

exercises.title: Use the shortcuts
tasks: A variable called lives is set to 3.
Use lives-- to lose one life. Log lives after.
A variable called coins is set to 0.
Use coins += 10 three separate times. Log coins after each one.
Then use coins *= 2 to double them. Log the final result.
exercises.hint: lives-- subtracts 1. coins += 10 adds 10. Both shortcuts work without needing the full version.

exercises.title: Score calculator
tasks: Start with score = 0.
Use += to add 50 points.
Use *= to triple it.
Use -= to subtract 25.
Use ++ to add 1 more.
Log 'Final score:' and score. The answer should be 126.
Then log whether the score is even or odd using %.
exercises.hint: Do each step one at a time. If you get stuck, add a console.log after each line to track your progress.

exercises.title: Build a math story
tasks: You start with 100 coins. Write each step as code.
You spend 20 coins at the shop.
You find a chest that doubles your coins.
You collect 10 more coins.
You split your coins equally with a friend (divide by 2).
Log 'You have X coins left.' at the end.
Bonus: log whether your final coin count is even or odd using %.
exercises.hint: Use -=, *=, += and /= for each step. Add a console.log after each operation to track the value.

challenges.title: The answer should be 20 not 14
challenges.description: This expression should calculate (2 + 3) * 4 = 20, but it's giving 14. One fix will do it.
challenges.hint: Think about what parentheses do to order of operations.
challenges.explanation: Without parentheses, * runs before +, so 3 * 4 = 12, then 2 + 12 = 14. To get 20, add parentheses: (2 + 3) * 4.

challenges.title: The score isn't updating correctly
challenges.description: score should be 100 after adding 50, but something is wrong. Find and fix it.
challenges.hint: Look carefully at line 2. Does it actually change score?
challenges.explanation: score + 50 calculates the result but throws it away it never stores it back into score. Fix: change it to score += 50 so score actually gets updated.

challenges.title: The discount isn't being applied
challenges.description: An item costs 80 and there's a 20% discount, so the final price should be 64. But the code gives the wrong answer. Find the bug.
challenges.hint: 20% means 20 out of 100, which is 0.20 as a decimal. What is price * 20 actually calculating?
challenges.explanation: price * 20 = 1600, not 16. To get 20% of a number you multiply by 0.20, not 20. Fix: change discount = price * 20 to discount = price * 0.20. Then discount = 16 and finalPrice = 80 - 16 = 64.
